WILL FIX BLAM f


FOR B0Y'SDEATH

pages.raw_text:
said that he was within 25 feet of
the boy before he noticed him and
blew the whistle. He then went
to the fireman's side of the cab and,
not seeing the boy any more,
thought he had escaped.


In the meantime a large crowd
had gathered, but for fully half an
hour no one could recognize the
remains. Two boys finally identi-
fed the corpse and while one went
to his home the other fastened to
the corner of Post street and
Sprague avenue, where the fathers
express wagon stood, and sum.
moned him to the scene.


When the police patrol arrived a
blanket was placed over the mutt
lated form. 1t was an effecting
scene that followed the lifting Of
the blanket when the father viewed
the remains of his son.


Whether or not an inquest will
be held will be determined this
afternoon by Coroner Witter, who
Is investigating the case and inter-
viewing witnesses and members of
the train crew. The coroner said.


"The question of the liability OF
the Northern Pacific company is
what l wish to determine before |
shall hold an inquest. II find the
engineer was lax In his duties and
did not blow whistle of warning
l shall call an inquest and for
mally place the blame upon him."
